entry.content: Thanks SreelathaN for great blog and all the links to SAP RBA. As an architect, I see tremendous value here: with SAP RBA and the expanded Reference Capability Model, we don’t start from scratch; we can utilize SAP's 50 years of industry experience and best practices. It lets us focus on differentiating choices rather than reinventing the baseline. If you are looking to set up your EA practice, this is perfect starting point 💎.

entry.title: Navigate to a Specified URL After Exiting a Performance Management Form
entry.content:
Hi Folks

Has anyone tested the functionality of returnURL for PM forms?
The document says, that we can configure the URL that the system takes us to, after we click on the Save and Close button, provided that we access the form through the deep link.

It also says that the deeplink to open a specific form will be something like https://hcm12preview.sapsf.eu/sf/pmreview?fid=364249&returnURL=www.abc.com

My question is that since the fid is unique for each form, it is technically impossible to put fid in the URL for all forms that we generate. 

Is there anyone who has tested this feature and can guide me?

Thanks in advance.

@#pmgm #peformance&goals

entry.title: SAP finance fiscal year changes
entry.content:
Hey, 


I'm new to SAP finance and looking for guidance on fiscal year configuration queries:

My book says "Only one open period at any point of time for posting. .... and then..... It is compulsory that at least two posting period intervals must be opened at any given time." (both statements appear contrary)

1. Would there be only one open period for posting at any point of time? If the earlier period is opened (the rule in second statement that requires atleast 2 to be open) how do we ensure that accountants do not post in the previous period? How is this done practically?  

2. If a company wants to change fiscal year variant assigned to a company code mid year, while the period is still open, what procedure should be followed & what must be taken care of? 

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Cloud Connector 2.19.0.2 is here!
entry.content:
New Cloud Connector feature releases are published as soon as the planned new feature development is completed and successfully tested. So, there is no fix release schedule, and depending on the complexity and development effort for the new features, this takes sometimes more time, and sometimes less. Looking into the past, the usual time frame between feature releases was 9-12 months. Patch releases between the feature releases depend on the severity and urgency of the fixed bugs. Usually, there are 2 or 3 patch releases between the feature releases. That makes a new version release approximately every 2-4 months.
But don't pin me down on these estimations. There might be exceptions in both directions.

entry.title: Future-Dated Records on Terminated Employees: 1H 2026 Finally Closes a Long-Standing Gap.
entry.content:
If you've worked in Employee Central long enough, you know this pattern. Someone gets terminated, but a future-dated promotion, comp change, or position move is sitting in their record. Payroll picks it up. Replication chokes. Someone files a ticket Monday morning. Repeat.

Customers have been raising this for years. The 1H 2026 release finally addresses it through enhancement ECT-260432, driven by Customer Idea 249640.

What Actually Changes

When you terminate an employee, the system now automatically deletes any future-dated Job Information and Compensation Information records dated after the termination date. Related Position Management data follows the same rule. Time Off records are not covered, so keep your Termination End Handling process running for those.

The cleanup runs whether the termination flows through the UI (Latest or Legacy People Profile, Action Search, or Manage My Team), an import, or an OData API call. Every auto-deleted record lands in the audit logs, so you don't lose visibility.

There's also a smaller related improvement. If an admin tries to create a data change record on an already-terminated employee, the system now warns that the target user is inactive but still allows the save, because there are legitimate cases for it.

The Scenarios This Finally Puts to Rest

The mid-cycle resignation. A promotion approved last month, effective next month. The employee resigns in between. Until now, that future record sat there, payroll picked it up, replication choked, someone wrote a P1 ticket on a Monday morning.

The merit cycle ghost. April 1 comp updates pushed across the org. One employee resigns mid-March. Until now, the comp record stayed put, finance reports overstated forward salary spend, and someone wrote a manual reconciliation script.

The fiscal-year position move. A position change scheduled for the new fiscal year. Employee leaves before it kicks in. Until now, ghost position data confused the org chart and your S/4HANA replication target until the next data audit caught it.

If you've lived through any of these, you know none of them are exotic edge cases. They're Tuesday.

Two Things to Know Before You Turn It On

1. The default depends on the event reason type. For non-status-changing event reasons, the cleanup is automatically enabled and no action is required.

For status-changing event reasons, the "Delete Future-Dated Records After Terminate Action" toggle in Manage Organization, Pay and Job Structures → Event Reason defaults to No.

To extend cleanup to those, flip it to Yes per event reason.

Audit your event reason configuration before going live. That's the only way to make sure you actually catch every case relevant to your org. Note: this field can't be included in reports, so the audit has to be done in the configuration UI.

2. It does not apply to changes made through the History UI. If your admins use History UI to back-date or correct termination records, future-dated records will not be touched.

You'll still need a manual sweep for those flows. Worth flagging to your support team before the release lands.

Why This One Matters

This won't show up in any Joule launch video. There's no demo with sparkles. But for anyone who has spent a Monday morning chasing down a payroll replication failure caused by a comp record on someone who left two weeks ago, this is the kind of fix that quietly buys back weekends.

Audit your event reasons. Update your termination runbook. And give your operations team the news.

entry.title: ABAP Objects: How to Properly Reset an Instance to Its Initial State
entry.content:
Hi everyone,
Enjoying my weekend coffee ☕️ and reflecting on a tricky debugging session I had this week. I spent 2 days chasing a bug related to object state, so today I want to share a quick tip on resetting an instance to its initial state in ABAP Objects.
The Solution:
To clear an object and free up memory, the simplest way is using the free() method.
Example:
lo_object_instance->free( ).
Why it matters:
In my case, the object was retaining old data from previous loops, leading to unexpected behavior. By explicitly calling free(), I ensured the instance was cleared before being reused or re-initialized.
It was a tough 2-day debug, but a great lesson learned!
Have a great weekend and happy coding! 💻

entry.title: Error Handling during CVI
entry.content:
As the deadline for moving to S4HANA is getting closer, more organizations are  thinking of doing a brownfield  or selective migration. One of the prerequisites for this is creation of Business partners (BP) for Customer & Vendors.

The business side of the organization needs to be heavily involved  across the various phases of  the CVI process.  IT teams need to closely collaborate along with Business user during initial stage when defining BP grouping, number ranges  based on business requirements as this forms the base and  ensures that new master data structure supports daily operations.

Business users are heavily involved in the data preparation &  validation stages as part of data cleansing , standardizing data/ address across customers & vendors. deduplication, merging of customer& vendors.

In the blog below I have listed some of the errors which are encountered and resolution steps which were taken to resolve them.

As any CVI process, the various required SAP Notes & configuration required for CVI were completed and then the synchronization activities were performed to create the BPs. Various errors were encountered during master data consistency check. Errors were resolved  in a combination of 3 approaches

  • Data correction at customer /vendor level
  • Configuration change 
  • Combination of data correction and configuration change

IT team will need to work along with Business users to resolve the errors.

Country Not Maintained

In Business partner (BP) country key is a mandatory field and needs to be populated before the CVI process. ex: Vendor 0099999011  does not have country maintained.

Solution: Country to be updated in Postal address for vendor

Postal Code is not Valid

ex: Postal code for below vendors is not valid

0040200781

0040203817

Solution: Update correct Postal code in  vendor.

Tax Jurisdiction Code WH00001 is incorrect for country PR.

ex: Tax Jurisdiction Code WH00001 is incorrect for country PR for customer 0001002871

Solution: Correct the Tax Jurisdiction Code in customer

Transport Zone is mandatory.

As the system configuration Transport Zone is mandatory field for customer and it is blank for almost 2943 customer.

Solution: The fix for this required both configuration changes and master data change

  • Create Configuration entry as per the required errors with required translations. ( we had to  maintain translation in French also)
    • Transportation Zone : Z_NO_TRANS
    • English Description :No Transportation
    • French Description : Pas de Transport

IMG-->Logistics Execution -->Shipping -->Basic Shipping Functions -->Routes -->Route Determination -->Define Transportation Zones

  • Update  transport zone in applicable customer records as per entries maintained in sheet

Tax Code 2 XXXXXXXX is incorrect for country FR.

Country Specific checks defined in system for country FR has  Other Data set  due to check report is giving error.

Solution: Remove flag from checkbox Other data for country FR as shown below

 Tcode OY17

Tax Type XXX is not allowed for country XX; check table TFKTAXNUMTYPE

Ex: Tax Type AD1 is not allowed for country AD; check table TFKTAXNUMTYPE
Tax Type AE1 is not allowed for country AE; check table TFKTAXNUMTYPE
Tax Type AE5 is not allowed for country AE; check table TFKTAXNUMTYPE

Error is due to Missing tax number categories maintained in the system.

Solution:  Missing tax number categories have be maintained in the system. Reference SAP Notes for this error are listed below.

775919 - Missing tax number categories of business partner

2021660 - Missing tax number categories in note 775919

2428946 - Error "tax number category ** does not exist in customizing" in BP (message no. FKBPTAX015)

Solution: Tcode SM30

Table/view: V_TFKTAXNUMTYPE

Tcode SM30

Table/view :V_TFKTAXNUMTYPEC

Note: Number of entries in both table TFKTAXNUMTYPE & TFKTAXNUMTYPEC should match.

Form of address 0001 not designated for organizations

CVI creates new Business Partners of type organization. The form of address is not allowed for organizations.

Error screenshot from MDS_PPO2 below

On checking the  customer record we see that  Title has Ms

Solution: IMG -> Cross-Application Components -> SAP Business Partner -> Business Partner -> Basic Settings -> Forms of Address -> Maintain Form of Address

Processing error again ( below Screenshots are sample screenshots for individual error record reprocessing) : Click on Ind. Synchronization button

Refresh the Screen , you will see green traffic light if error is processed successfully.

No geocoder for country XX maintained in system (table GEOCODERS)

Countries listed in the error are

  • RS
  • BL
  • MF

Solution:

  • Put check mark in maintenance table GEOCUST so that during synchronization, the table GEOCODERS will not be called.

Tcode SM30

 Table GEOCUST

  • Maintain and create entry for the concerned country code in table GEOCODERS.

Tcode SM30 Table GEOCODERS

Maintain customizing entry for the 3 countries

IMG --> SAP NetWeaver--> General settings--> Set Geocoding --> Process Geographical Data for Countries-->Process Geographical Data for Countries

Maintain 0 in both degree

Reprocess the errors, the errors should be processed successfully .

Time zone CETW does not exist

Customer  1003337 gives error message Time zone CETW does not exist

Solution:

There were 2 default entries for FO. CETW time zone does not exist, Delete the entry for FO and let the association with WET time zone active

 Update the customizing

IMG--> SAP NetWeaver--> General settings --> Time Zones -->Maintain Geographical Assignments

Update timezone in customer  1003337

Entry in field Tax Code 2 is longer than 11 characters

Sample error message : 40201885           CVI_02  Vendor -> Business Partner         Entry in field Tax Code 2 is longer than 11 characters

Solution: Reference  SAP Note 2098239 - Entry in field tax code 2 is longer than 11 characters

Change the error message AR103 to a Warning or Info message

Tcode OBA5

Application Area : AR

Reprocess the errors for tax code.

Errors during merging vendors & customers into single BP

Ensure the Notes for merging are applied in system.
• 2363892 - Linking Customer and Vendor to a single Business Partner during initial load before upgrade to S/4
• 2968536 - Harmonize the behavior for Linking Customer and Vendor to a single Business Partner

Prerequisite for synchronizing of vendors and customers in to single BP is
• As a The Customer and Vendor must be linked through the Customer number field in the Vendor table and the Vendor number field in the Customer table.
• The Customer and Vendor must contain exactly the same Postal Address information.
• If the Customer and Vendor have the same Bank Account information (same Country, Bank Key and Bank Account), then the remaining fields for this entry must also match.

Refer to SAP NOTE 2716945 : This note contains list of various fields which SAP checks internally during merging.

Multiple email address

Email address should match for customer and vendor. Customers had multiple email address maintained while only single email is maintained in vendor master

Solution: Update vendor master records with all email address maintained in customer master

Language Mismatch

Language for vendor & customer should be identical.

example :RU10  has vendor language as Hebrew, while it is English in  Customer record.

Solution: Update Vendor language to English

Communication Mismatch

Communication value for customer & vendor should match.

example: Error for vendor MY10

Solution: Update communication mode in vendor same as customer.

Bank Detail Mismatch

Bank details for vendor & customer should be identical.

Example : In our case Vendor records had bank details maintained while customer records didn’t have bank account updated.

SI10

PT10

 Solution: As customer records were already synchronized with BP, the customer records were updated with bank details same as vendor.

Address creation language Mismatch

During synchronizing customer & vendor into single BP, SAP checks multiple fields and one of the fields is address creation language.

Due to mismatch between the customer & vendor records the following vendors had errors

AU10

MY10

TH10

Solution: As per blog on CVI some fields like LANGU_CR cannot even be changed in the UI. Also no  SAP note available to update the value in field.

Showing steps performed for AU10 as reference.

We needed to  manually edit the table ADRC for these records and update the field with customer language AU10

Vendor-AU10-Address number =91468-->Customer-AU10-Address number =55010

Data before change

Function module used SE16N_INTERFACE

Selection screen for FM

Output of Function Module

Change language to FR

 

Changes after saving.

Reprocessing AU10  created the BP.

Refer to SAP Note 974504 (974504 - Inconsistencies in link tables of master data sync)  for cleanup on CVI_VEND_LINK table as required

The above listed are some of the errors which were encountered across different CVI performed.

The CVI process should be started in a sandbox enviorement which is a copy of Production data. all the corrections especially data related should be done in parallel in the Production as it will help improve the data quality in Production and number of errors reported in production would be low.

This is not an exhaustive list and it depends a lot on the data quality maintained in ECC system by Business. Hence close collaboration between IT teams & Business users is required.

entry.title: Understanding SAP Supply Chain through Production, Procurement and Sales by AI Comics
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • The Trigger: Planning (From Demand to Decision)
  • Flow I: Procurement & VIM (From Requirement to Settlement)
  • Flow II: Production & Logistics (From Raw Input to Available Output)
  • A Final Thought on the Ecosystem

Introduction

My SAP career journey didn't start in IT; it started on the ground. Four years ago, as a Supply Chain Officer, I tracked physical products across plants and warehouses, translating real-world logistics into the system. It taught me a fundamental truth: SAP doesn’t always drive the business—it captures it.

Later on, my career transitioned to SAP supply chain & VIM functional consultant, there, the whole SAP supply chain ecosystem completes. This article is a periodic reflection of how I understand supply chain framework through SAP lens, from an essential perspective.

From moving raw materials to clearing final invoices, I learned to see SAP not as isolated transactions, but as one living ecosystem bridging the physical and financial worlds. To understand how SAP truly connects material movement to financial settlement, you only need to master three core flows: Procurement, Production, and Sales.

  1. Procurement brings inputs into the business.
  2. Production transforms those inputs into semi-finished or finished goods.
  3. Sales delivers the final output to customers and converts delivery into revenue.

Here is how they tie everything together (I will explain how each flow operates in SAP S/4HANA system):

After the overview graph, I will explain each flow by key transactions, SAP Document via Comics generated by AI 🙂

 

 

The Trigger: Planning (From Demand to Decision)

Before a single material moves or an invoice is paid, there is demand. Whether it comes from a customer order, a forecast, or safety stock, the supply chain needs a spark.

At this stage, the goal isn't execution—it’s decision-making. In SAP S/4HANA, Material Requirements Planning (MRP) takes the business demand and compares it against existing stock. If there’s a shortage, SAP asks one fundamental question: Do we make it, or do we buy it?

  • If we make it: MRP generates a Planned Order (the intent), which later becomes a Process Order (the execution).

  • If we buy it: MRP generates a Purchase Requisition (the request), which later becomes a Purchase Order (the agreement).

The Technical Toolkit:

  • Execution: MD01N / MD01 / MD02 (Run MRP)

  • Visibility: MD04 (Stock/Requirements), MMBE (Stock Overview), Monitor Material Coverage (Fiori)

Flow I: Procurement & VIM (From Requirement to Settlement)

When we decide to buy, we enter the Procurement flow. This is where external materials or services enter our ecosystem.

On the ground, this flow is about physical receipt. In the system, it is the bridge between goods movement and financial liability. A buyer issues the Purchase Order, and later, the warehouse receives the physical goods.

But the flow doesn't end at the loading dock. When the vendor’s invoice arrives, it isn't just a finance document; it must be reconciled against what actually happened on the ground. This is where OpenText Vendor Invoice Management (VIM) steps in. VIM captures, validates, and matches the invoice against the system's evidence before any money leaves the company.

The Golden Rule of Procurement:

  • PO: What we agreed to buy

  • GR (Goods Receipt): What physically arrived

  • Invoice (VIM): What we are being charged for

  • Payment: The settled liability

The Technical Toolkit:

  • Procurement: ME51N (Create PR), ME21N (Create PO)

  • Logistics: MIGO (Goods Receipt), MB03/MB51 (Material Documents)

  • Finance/VIM: VIM Workplace, MIRO (Enter Invoice), F110 (Payment Run)

Flow II: Production & Logistics (From Raw Input to Available Output)

When we decide to make, we enter the Production flow. Also if Procurement brings materials into the warehouse, Production is what consumes them. In a manufacturing environment, a Process Order dictates exactly what raw materials are needed to create a finished product.

When the warehouse issues components to the Process Order, SAP records that inventory has been consumed. When manufacturing finishes, a Goods Receipt is posted, and a new, finished product officially enters the system.

However, "technically received" in SAP doesn't always mean "ready to sell." The physical stock may require quality release, or it might need to be moved to a distribution center. This triggers a Stock Transport Order (STO) and an Outbound Delivery, ensuring the physical product is positioned exactly where the business needs it next.

The Golden Rule of Production & Movement:

  • Goods Issue: Components consumed by the factory

  • Goods Receipt: Finished product enters the system

  • STO & Delivery: Moving the physical stock to the right location

The Technical Toolkit:

  • Production: COR1 (Create Process Order), COR6N (Confirm Order), MIGO (Issue/Receipt)

  • Movement: ME21N (Create STO), VL10B (Create Delivery), VL02N (Picking/Packing/PGI)

Flow III: Sales (From Customer Order to Collected Revenue)

Once the final product is ready, the Sales flow takes over. This is where the physical supply chain converts into revenue.

It starts with a Customer Purchase Order, which SAP translates into a Sales Order. The warehouse takes over to pick, pack, and ship the product. The most critical step here is the Post Goods Issue (PGI)—this is the moment SAP recognizes that the physical inventory has officially left your control.

Only after the goods have shipped does the finance cycle close the loop. A Billing Document is generated, turning into a customer invoice and an open receivable. Once the customer pays, the account is cleared.

The Golden Rule of Sales:

  • Sales Order: What the customer requested

  • PGI: The exact moment inventory leaves our hands

  • Billing: What we ask the customer to pay

  • Incoming Payment: The closed loop

The Technical Toolkit:

  • Sales & Logistics: VA01 (Create Sales Order), VL01N/VL02N (Outbound Delivery & PGI)

  • Finance: VF01 (Create Billing Document), F-28 (Post Incoming Payment)

*****************************************************************************************************************************************

A Final Thought on the Ecosystem

The most critical takeaway is that the SAP supply chain does not exist in a vacuum. It is a circular ecosystem.

When you look at the "End-to-End" flow, you realize that every action in your system triggers a reaction in another.

A Purchase Order (PO) sent to a vendor often acts as the digital trigger that automatically creates a Sales Order (SO) in their SAP system via EDI (Electronic Data Interchange).

The loop closes with a perfect symmetry:

The Seller’s Output: Your SD Billing Document is the final step of your Sales flow, creating a customer receivable - vendor invoice for the customer.

By mastering these three flows—Procurement, Production, and Sales—you aren't just learning how to move materials or click through T-codes. You are managing a continuous, circular ecosystem where physical movements create financial obligations, and one company’s "Goods Issue" becomes another’s "Goods Receipt."

This SAP supply chain ecosystem  ensures when the physical product stops moving, the financial ledger is already settled.

entry.title: Re: Unifying ATC Check Variants in SAP Cloud ALM for Feat...
entry.content:
Hello,
Regarding the Feature Export Check, please check in
https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/transport-checks?locale=en-US#prerequisites-for-export-checks
that the need to set the Behavior on Release of the Code Inspector to Disable "Code Inspector" as test driver.

Regarding the Clean Core Dashboard, see that in the RISE with SAP Methodology you can see the System View dashboard where ATC is one of the tools that you use
to collect clean-core-related data.
You must manually import the check results generated in the ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC). First, create the check result for your selected system in the ATC. Then, download the check result file and import it into the dashboard.
See this described in https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/system-view?locale=en-US#data-collection-tools

In https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/extensibility?locale=en-US#before-you-start pay attention to the Prerequisites section where it is described how you need to
"Generate an ATC variant for clean core with the following three checks:
Allowed SAP enhancement technologies
Usage of APIs
Critical statements
..."

Hope this helps,
Dolores

entry.title: Re: abapgit-agent: AI Coding Assistants for ABAP
entry.content:
It's been two months since I introduced the abapgit-agent. Here's a summary of what landed since then (v1.10 → v1.19.1):

New Commands

run — Execute an ABAP program or a class implementing IF_OO_ADT_CLASSRUN directly from the terminal — no SAP GUI needed

abapgit-agent run --class ZCL_MY_RUNNER

transport — List, create, check, and release SAP transport requests

abapgit-agent transport list
abapgit-agent transport release DEVK900001

lint — Run abaplint static analysis on changed files, offline, no ABAP connection needed

abapgit-agent lint --base main

drop — Physically delete a single ABAP object from the system (useful before a clean re-pull)

abapgit-agent drop --files src/zcl_broken.clas.abap --pull

customize — Write a row to a SAP customizing table (delivery class C/E)

abapgit-agent customize ZAPP_CONFIG --set KEY=FEATURE_X VALUE=active

Smarter Pull

  • Conflict detection — Pull now detects branch switches and object conflicts before activating, configurable via .abapgit-agent.json
  • Async pull for large changesets — Pulls with >10 files automatically execute via a background job to avoid HTTP timeouts
  • Transport selector — Choose which transport request to use, with hook support for CI pipelines
  • --sync-xml — After pull, syncs XML metadata back to local files

Better Testing & CI

  • unit now uses the ADT API — Richer output: test class name, failure details, execution time per method
  • Coverage threshold enforcement — Define minimum coverage % in .abapgit-agent.json; CI gate fails if not met
  • --junit-output on both inspect and unit — Emit JUnit XML for Jenkins/GitHub Actions integration
  • inspect improvements — Variant from project config, exclude/suppress rules, chunked requests for large file sets, and --doc to fetch SCI check documentation inline

Deeper Debugging

  • Debug --expand TABLE[N] — Expand nested table variables and field symbols during a debug session
  • Scripted debug scenarios — AI agents can now drive debug sessions end-to-end: set breakpoint → attach → step → inspect → continue
  • In-process debug daemon — More reliable attach/detach; no more frozen SM50 work processes after REPL disconnect

More Object Types

view and pull now support: DOMA, MSAG, FUGR, DCLS, SUSO (authorization objects), ENHO (enhancement implementations), and XML-only objects (TABL, STRU, DTEL, TTYP)

Quality-of-Life

  • abgagt shortcut alias (saves keystrokes)
  • Auto-recovery from expired SAP sessions (no more manual re-auth mid-workflow)
  • --version / -v flag
  • --verbose flag for HTTP error body details
  • view --full --lines for dual line numbers (global + include-relative) used by AI to set precise breakpoints
  • Bundled guidelines + guide --migrate — The ABAP development guide is now bundled inside the CLI instead of copied into your repo as numbered files (guidelines/01_..., guidelines/02_..., etc.). If you're on an older project, run:
    abapgit-agent guide --migrate
    This removes the old guideline files, replaces CLAUDE.md and copilot-instructions.md with slim stubs, and keeps any project-specific files you had under guidelines/. The full guide is always available on demand via abapgit-agent guide.

Install / Upgrade

npm install -g abapgit-agent@latest
abapgit-agent init --update

init --update refreshes your CLAUDE.md with guidance for all new commands.

Full docs: https://sylvoscai.github.io/abapgit-agent/

entry.title: Commodity Management in the Chemical Industry: Why It Matters and How SAP Addresses It
entry.content:
Chemical companies - whether they produce fertilizers, petrochemicals, polymers, or industrial acids - operate in one of the most volatile and financially complex sectors of the global economy. Unlike typical manufacturing businesses, where raw material prices are relatively stable and procurement is straightforward, the chemical industry depends on commodities whose prices can change dramatically from one day to the next.

Managing this complexity is not just a logistical challenge - it is a strategic imperative. That is where Commodity Management comes in, and why SAP has built a dedicated suite of capabilities directly into its ERP platform to address it.

What Is Commodity Management?

Commodity Management is a specialized set of modules and functions designed to handle the unique complexities of procuring, pricing, trading, and managing commodity raw materials and finished goods - goods whose prices are subject to market volatility.

A commodity is a good that is actively resold on organised global markets, including stock exchanges. Commodities are characterised by:

  • Large trading volumes (tonnes, cubic meters)
  • Global standardisation and interchangeability
  • Prices driven by the balance of global supply and demand
  • A high proportion of forward and futures transactions
  • Significant stock exchange turnover

For the chemical industry specifically, commodities include acids, ammonia, monomers, basic polymers, and fertilizers - all of which are inputs or outputs of complex, high-volume production processes that span multiple continents and supply chain tiers.

Key insight: While standard procurement handles buying and selling finished or semi-finished goods at relatively stable, negotiated prices, commodity management deals with the reality of fluctuating market prices, complex logistics, and significant financial risk - all at the same time.

Why Is Commodity Management a Distinct Business Discipline?

Five core characteristics make commodity management fundamentally different from standard supply chain and procurement operations:

  • 1 Price Volatility
    The price of a raw material like naphtha, ammonia, or potassium chloride can shift by the minute. Contracts cannot simply carry a fixed price — they must be linked to a market index, such as "LME Copper + differential." Companies that fail to account for this exposure put their margins at serious risk
  • 2 Complex Pricing Mechanisms 
    Commodity pricing is rarely a single number. It typically involves a provisional price set at the time of shipment and a final price determined later — once quality analysis is complete, delivery is confirmed, and market conditions for a specific averaging period are known. The formula can be multilayered, incorporating index averages, differentials, quality bonuses or penalties, and surcharges.
  • 3 Financial Risk Exposure 
    Price fluctuations create major financial exposure. A company that purchases a commodity today for future delivery and faces a market price drop has overpaid — potentially significantly. Managing this exposure through financial instruments such as futures, options, forwards, and swaps is a core discipline in commodity-driven businesses.
  • 4 Regulatory Compliance and Complex Accounting 
    Trading commodities — especially when financial derivatives are involved — requires strict regulatory oversight and adherence to complex accounting standards such as Mark-to-Market (MtM) revaluation and IFRS hedge accounting rules.
  • 5 Multi-Modal Bulk Logistics 
    Commodities are moved in bulk: by vessel, pipeline, rail, and truck. Differences between the loaded quantity and quality and what is received at the destination directly affect the final invoice. Capturing these actualisations accurately is essential for correct financial settlement.

A few numbers

  • 65% of companies do not have, or are unsure they have, the correct technology to face current market volatility
  • 30% cite control and transparency as a primary driver for technology change
  • 30% cite competitive advantage as a key driver for upgrading their CTRM platform

Source: Commodities People Energy Trading Annual Survey 2023

The Chemical Industry Value Chain: Where Commodity Management Operates

Chemical production chains are long and complex. Consider a fertilizer producer. The process starts with the mining and enrichment of mineral raw materials such as phosphorus and potassium. These are then transported to chemical plants, combined with gases (nitrogen, sulphur), processed through acids production, and ultimately formulated into complex fertilizer products (NPK, NPKS). Each of these steps involves commodity purchases, multi-modal logistics, and price-sensitive contracts.

Similarly, in petrochemicals, naphtha sourced from oil refineries feeds cracking plants that produce monomers like ethylene and propylene - which in turn become polymers, plastics, and downstream consumer products. At every step, there are commodity contracts, market-priced inputs, shipping logistics, and risk exposure that must be actively managed.

Commodity Management applies across two primary zones in these value chains:

  • Procurement side: buying raw materials at market-linked prices from external suppliers and managing the price risk between contract signing and final delivery
  • Sales side: selling finished goods at commodity-indexed prices to global customers and distributors, managing delivery terms, quality adjustments, and final invoicing

SAP's Solution Portfolio for Commodity Management

SAP offers what it describes as the world's only commodity management solution built directly into an ERP platform. This embedded approach eliminates the need for costly, complex integrations between separate CTRM (Commodity Trading and Risk Management) tools and the ERP system - replacing a fragmented landscape of spreadsheets, risk tools, logistics systems, and ERP with a single platform.

The core principle: One platform. One source of truth. End-to-end control from trade to cash.

The SAP solution portfolio for commodity management spans several integrated components:

CM for Physical Contracts

Manages commodity procurement and sales contracts with market-linked pricing, provisional and final invoicing, quality-driven price adjustments, and period-end revaluation. Fully embedded in SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP Private.

Commodity Pricing Engine (CPE)

Enables complex, formula-driven pricing rules linked to market data. Supports fixed, indexed, combination, differential, quality-adjusted, and partial-quantity pricing - all within a single flexible framework.

CM Risk Management

Provides exposure management, hedge management, Mark-to-Market analysis, Value at Risk (VaR), and full hedge accounting support compliant with IFRS standards. Uses capabilities of SAP Treasury and Risk Management.

Global Trade Management (GTM)

Manages end-to-end trading document flows including trading contracts, sales and purchase orders, document links via the Trading Execution Workbench, and position management by trading contracts.

SAP for Oil & Gas / TSW (Option for Oil and Gas Companies)

The Trading Scheduler Workbench (TSW) handles bulk logistics: nomination and ticketing, inventory planning, supply chain visualisation, berth planning, 3-way pegging, and integration with commodity management for risk exposure capture.

SAP Transportation Management (Option for Chemical, Mill Products, Mining and Agriculture Companies)

Provides Logistics Planning and Execution capabilities for Chemical Producers including Bulk Transportation. Compatible  

Global Trade Services (GTS)

Manages compliance, import/export controls, sanctioned and denied party screening, Intrastat, and broker communications - all natively integrated with SAP S/4HANA ERP, EWM and TM.

These components operate within the broader SAP Cloud ERP Private architecture, which integrates commodity management with Source-to-Pay (MM), Order-to-Cash (SD), warehousing, transportation, finance and accounting, cash management, and treasury - all coordinated through the Business Technology Platform

 

 

What Makes SAP's Approach Unique?

The fundamental differentiator is native integration. Commodity data - prices, risk positions, contract terms, logistics actualisations - flows in real time through a single data model. There is no reconciliation of separate systems, no duplicate master data, no complex interfaces to maintain between a standalone CTRM tool and the ERP. This delivers three key outcomes:

  • Profitability: Full transparency and accurate invoicing through the commodity pricing engine, increased visibility into risk and hedge performance, and improved accounting and costing compliance.
  • Integration: A single set of master data, transactions, and prices across all functions - procurement, trading, logistics, risk, and finance - with workflow and role-based security throughout.
  • Transparency: Real-time commodity supply chain planning, better and faster decision-making through commodity analytics, and a consistent, uninterrupted flow from logistics to risk to finance.
AI Feature

AI-Powered Capabilities: The Intelligent Commodity Work Center

SAP has introduced AI-assisted capabilities directly within commodity management workflows, powered by SAP Joule, SAP's generative AI copilot. These capabilities are designed to accelerate deal creation, reduce manual effort, and help traders work more efficiently.

The AI-Assisted Commodity Work Center (ACW) supports the following:

  • Draft document generation: Joule can create draft deal documents for transaction types including Purchase, Sale, and Buy & Sell - as well as Agricultural Contract Management (ACM) trading contracts for 3rd Party Purchase and 3rd Party Sales.
  • Multi-item and formula-based pricing support: Traders can instruct Joule to generate drafts with one or multiple items, using fixed prices or index/formula-based pricing.
  • Search by field similarity: The AI identifies similar documents already in the system based on key deal fields, allowing traders to leverage previous contracts as the basis for new ones - reducing setup time significantly.
  • SAP Fiori interface: The work center is built as a modern Fiori app, delivering a personalised, responsive, and intuitive user experience optimised for efficient trading operations.

Looking further ahead, Joule agents are positioned to orchestrate end-to-end commodity business processes - from deal capture through to logistics execution and financial settlement - within the SAP Cloud ERP Private architecture.

Customer Adoption in the Chemical Industry

SAP's commodity management solutions are already deployed across leading chemical companies globally. Customers such  NOVA Chemicals and others are live on SAP Commodity Management and reporting on business benefits. These real-world deployments validate the maturity and scalability of SAP's approach for the chemical sector.

Why Now?

The economic and geopolitical landscape of the 2020s has made commodity risk management a board-level priority. Supply chain disruptions, energy price shocks, currency volatility, and tightening environmental regulations have all amplified the importance of having accurate, real-time visibility into commodity exposure. The survey data cited above reflects an industry that knows it has a technology gap - and is actively seeking to close it.

SAP's commodity management portfolio - embedded in S/4HANA, co-innovated with leading chemical producers, and increasingly enhanced with AI - is positioned to close that gap for the chemical industry.

Key Takeaways

Commodity Management is a mission-critical business discipline for chemical producers and traders. It addresses price volatility, complex formula-driven pricing, financial risk exposure, compliance requirements, and multi-modal bulk logistics — all in an integrated way.

SAP offers the only commodity management solution natively embedded in an enterprise ERP, combining physical contract management, a commodity pricing engine, risk management, bulk logistics (TSW or Transportation Management), and global trade compliance into a single platform on SAP S/4HANA.

AI-powered capabilities through SAP Joule are now accelerating trader workflows, with AI-assisted deal creation and similarity-based document search available in the Commodity Work Center. And more of AI capabilities to come - please stay tuned!

More Information:

SAP Commodity Management, SAP for Chemicals

 
Sergey Nozhenko, SAP Industry Business Unit – Chemicals

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
entry.content:
Hi,

I chose RabbitMQ as my broker because it is lightweight, reliable, and highly portable and I could run locally via Docker. Since I am building this on a personal Linux environment, I wanted a stack that allows for full offline development while remaining compliant with the Cloud Events specification.

Using the MQTT plugin for RabbitMQ allows bridging traditional messaging with a Python-based pipeline.
This setup feeds directly into my workflow using Ollama (which I already had from previous experiments) for local embeddings and ChromaDB for vector storage, demonstrating how SAP-style event patterns can be implemented with an entirely local, open-source toolchain.

Here is the schema that I am using, I intentionally kept it simple.

When I had everything running in background I could launch the events:

These were then picked up and processed

Kind Regards,
Joery

entry.title: Re: Navigate to a Specified URL After Exiting a Performan...
entry.content:
Hi @adeeb_rizvi1,

I've tested this extensively, and you've hit on the exact pain point: manual vs. dynamic links.

From a Systems Engineering perspective, you should never manually construct these URLs. The 'Clean Core' way to handle this is through Tokens. If you add &returnURL=www.yourlink.com to the end of the [[DOC_ACCESS_URL]] token in your E-mail Notification Templates, SuccessFactors will do the heavy lifting of injecting the unique fid for every single user.

One tip from my research: Always ensure your returnURL is URL-encoded (e.g., use %3A%2F%2F instead of ://). If the system sees unencoded characters, the redirect often fails and defaults back to the standard Performance Inbox.

Quick question: Are you planning to send these links via email, or are you hosting them on an internal company dashboard?

entry.title: Re: SI3: Logistics_WM 兼容性包问题
entry.content:
您好 @evae ，

我的朋友，如果您的 SAP 就绪性检查显示 SI3：物流_WM (LE-WM) 受影响，则表示您的系统仍在使用 S/4HANA 兼容包中的仓库管理 (WM) 功能，而这些兼容包正在逐步淘汰。要解决此问题，您必须将 LE-WM 替换为 SAP 支持的替代方案，并在兼容性到期日之前停止使用 WM 事务。

要解决此问题，请按以下步骤操作：

确定仍在使用的 WM 功能。使用 SAP 就绪性检查 → 兼容性范围分析和 SAP EarlyWatch Alert → 兼容性使用到期报告（列出过去 3 个月使用的 WM 事务）。这将告诉您哪些 WM 流程必须替换。

选项 1 — 迁移到库存室管理 (SRM)

SRM 是 LE-WM 的“技术继任者”，用于基本仓储管理。它包含在 S/4HANA 中，无需额外许可证。

如果您仅使用 WM 进行上架、拣货、调拨单等基本仓库流程，则此方案适用。

方案二 — 升级到扩展仓库管理 (EWM)

如果您使用 WM 的高级功能，例如波次管理、堆场管理、货位分配、劳动力管理以及复杂的入库/出库流程，则必须升级到 EWM。

EWM 的部署方式：嵌入式 EWM、分散式 EWM

希望这能帮助您解决问题。如果我的解决方案对您有帮助，请采纳。如果您有任何疑问，欢迎随时提问。

entry.title: Re: Is it possible to show only leaves in SAC Excel add-in table?
entry.content: Hi, I’ve run into something similar before. Unfortunately the SAC Excel Add-in doesn’t currently offer the same “Show Leaves Only” behavior that’s available in SAC stories. In Excel, the hierarchy expansion is handled differently, so parent nodes like “ALL” and “2025” often still appear even if you only want the month members. One workaround is to flatten or filter the hierarchy level directly in the model/query if possible, or use member filtering to restrict the dimension to the lowest level only. Some people also disable hierarchy display entirely in the table settings when they only need month-level values. I recently read an article about [AI call agents and business process automation](https://flowster.app/ai-call-agents-the-next-frontier-in-business-process-automation/) and it reminded me how many enterprise tools still handle hierarchy and automation logic differently across interfaces — web apps vs add-ins can behave surprisingly inconsistently. Hope you find a clean workaround!

entry.title: Re: Extensibility options for SAP Real Estate in S/4HANA...
entry.content:
Dear @Magda_RR,

In Public Cloud only the Contract and Lease Management (CLM) part of classic RE-FX is in scope. Classic RE master data Business Entity, Property, Building, Rental Unit, Pooled Space is not part of Public Edition SAP's strategic solution for that side is Planon Real Estate and Facilities Management for SAP which became an SAP Solution Extension in January 2026. The limited number of Business Contexts and released Badis you see is a consequence of that scoping

More details below:

https://community.sap.com/t5/financial-management-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-real-estate-and-facilities-management/ba-p/14310012

On your first point recommended extensibility approaches for FI-impact RE scenarios. The supported model is the standard three tiers Key User Extensibility (Custom Fields, Custom Logic against released Business Contexts), Developer Extensibility (Embedded Steampunk, ABAP Cloud, 3-system landscape) and Side-by-Side on SAP BTP. User-exits and classic modifications are not supported (clean core SAP Note 3578329). In practice for FI impact you work in this order configuration first (expense types, account determination, valuation parameters) then cloud Badis contract-side RECN_CONTRACT_CHECK_ALL / RECN_CONTRACT_SUBSTITUTE / RECE_VALUATION_PARAMETER, FI-side the standard journal entry Badis in the Custom Logic app under Finance / Posting and finally the OData posting API for anything outside a Badi hook

More details below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/3578329

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_CLOUD_FOR_REAL_ESTATE/1ccb507ddc6b4191bc1695c2d2995af5/cc59e124fed04627aee1eeaf2e91d53b.html

On your second point released APIs, Badis and events for RE beyond what is in the system. The contract-side Badis you have already found are essentially the delivered toolkit. There is no separate set of RE-specific accounting Badis FI-impact logic goes through the standard FI extension surface. The relevant delivery on the FI side is the Accounting Business Transaction Interface introduced in 2502, with configuration items Define Real Estate Expense Type (106882) and Define Account Determination (106906) and the OData V4 API Real Estate Cost Allocation Accounting Notification Post Journal Entry for posting from external or side-by-side logic, traceable in Manage Accounting Notification (F7289). Release 2508 extended cost settlement across architectural structures, usable/rentable objects and CLM contracts. No RE-specific business events are published on Business Accelerator Hub today

More details below:

https://community.sap.com/t5/financial-management-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-contract-and-lease-mgmt-product-amp-strategy-updates-latest-innovation/ba-p/14370862

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/finance-with-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-2502/ba-p/13957394

https://api.sap.com/package/S4HANACloudBADI/overview

https://api.sap.com/products/SAPS4HANACloud/overview

On your third point roadmap and best-practice documentation for customer-specific accounting logic. The canonical reference is Implementing Contract and Lease Management in an SAP S/4HANA Cloud System on SAP Help Portal, together with the relevant scope items (1T6, 21P, 34N, 21Q, 2SA, 2SB, 34O, 2O9, 7EZ, 7G4, 7G5, 21R) and the 2502 / 2508 FI and CLM release notes. On the roadmap side the direction publicly delivered so far is the Accounting Business Transaction Interface and the cost settlement extension anything beyond that is best tracked in SAP Roadmap Explorer

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/70747b45a7ab457b8eafbf4474a20d0c/b7138d1ebe19469a889137bc1abd3ae5.html

On your fourth point the missing Business Context for RE objects as Usable Objects. This follows from the scoping decision above. The classic RE master data layer is not in scope of Public Edition, so a Business Context exposing it for reuse is not delivered. What is exposed Rentable Object, CLM Contract, contract objects is available via released CDS views and OData services visible in ADT and on Business Accelerator Hub. There is no public commitment to deliver an RE master data Business Context if the requirement is concrete and recurring the right channel is SAP Customer Influence campaign 2759

More details below:

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/2759

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Technical Inquiry on Account Differentiation via BADI...
entry.content:
Dear @S0024835231,

BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD is a supported Public Cloud BAdI for Purchase Order Accruals, but its scope is the final Accrual Engine journal entry line item, not the original PO item. SAP confirms that this BAdI can be used to modify journal entry line fields such as G/L account, cost center and profit center for PO Accruals postings

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3068511

For this reason, I would not position BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD as the enhancement point to directly read PO item attributes such as Product Type Group / Policy Type. If the account logic must depend on such PO-level information, this value must first be available in the Accrual Engine context. SAP shows the same design pattern for PO-derived information such as Trading Partner the value is brought into the Accrual Engine using custom field / field mapping logic and then used during Accrual Engine posting

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3256557

For PO Accruals logic that depends on PO item classification or whether an accrual object/item should be created the more relevant enhancement points are the PO Accruals-specific BAdIs for example POAC_ACCRUAL_OBJECT_ITEMS and POAC_PO_ITEM_CLASSIFICATION. SAP confirms POAC_ACCRUAL_OBJECT_ITEMS for influencing PO accrual object items in Public Cloud

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3544598

Conclusion:

BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD can be used to dynamically override the final debit/credit G/L account on Accrual Engine posting lines. It should not be used as the main PO item classification BAdI. For Policy Type / Product Type Group-based account logic the recommended design is to derive or map the policy value into the Accrual Engine context first, and then use BADI_ACE_DOCUMENT_SCHEMA_CLD only for the final G/L account adjustment

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Acing the SAP Roles and Authorization Design Review — What SAP Checks and How to Prepare
entry.content:
Treat your Non-Prod systems like production. Because, SAP certainly will.

We recently had a security design review done by SAP itself, and we came out with positive feedback on almost all parameters. To be honest, I was a little anxious before the report, but we had been doing everything right, so it went well. I tried to find what SAP checks before the review, but couldn't find much; hence writing this blog. This information will come in handy while prepping for a Design Review. Fair warning — if you follow all of this outside of review season too, your system will be embarrassingly clean. People will start asking what your secret is. Just smile and point them here.

Start taking care of all these things from the QA system onwards. Whatever system, SAP performs review on, they treat it as Prod system and test all parameters, and sometimes it becomes really hard to make the client understand that couple profile assignments to service users/systems users is fine 🙂  particularly the clients who are new to SAP and traditionally had one environment for their ERPs (trust me, I have seen those).

I'll go one by one, divided into 7 sections:

  1. Governance
  2. User Administration
  3. Role Administration
  4. Fiori Launchpad Administration
  5. Custom Transactions
  6. Authorization-based SAP S/4HANA Enterprise Management
  7. Miscellaneous

1. Governance

  1. Authorization Concept Documentation - No matter what phase you are in, always have the documentation updated. Like your user management policy, role design documents -> design and the role naming principles etc. It can be as simple as couple slides, but having that is a must. A good role design document should cover the full picture: what roles exist and what access they give, your naming convention, your list of critical accesses and controls. Make sure you maintain version history, segregated sections for Master, Derived and Composite Roles. My advice: don't create this document for the review. Create it as soon as you start the design, update it as your design evolves, and make sure it's accessible to key stakeholders. That's what SAP wants to see.
  2. Naming Convention - I am not going to say that, you should use ZD or Z_D for derived roles. Use anything, but it has to be consistent. If you've been disciplined about this (e.g., prefixes that distinguish security roles, display roles, composite roles, etc.), you're fine. This one is easy to get right if you define the convention early and enforce it. Don't let people create ad-hoc role names mid-project.

2. User Administration

  1. Master Roles Assigned to Users - Master roles are templates — they should never be directly assigned to users. Only derived roles go on users. SAP checks this explicitly. If your project has been following a proper derivation concept, you'll have no findings here.
  2. Direct Profile Assignments — Especially SAP_ALL and SAP_NEW - SAP looks for users who have profiles assigned directly (outside of PFCG roles), and specifically calls out SAP_ALL and SAP_NEW. In QA environments, teams often give these to BASIS team members while roles are still being built. I would suggest a better one, until you have the matured roles for basis, create one role from SAP_ALL template, deactivate Sec related objects. May be call this Y*SAP_ALL*MINUS_SECURITY, but use it until you have enough inputs to create one support/FF role. A word of caution on interface users; these are often the ones that get forgotten. SAP_ALL on a system or service user is actually more dangerous than on a dialog user, because it opens the door for RFC-based attacks. Ideally you should have minimum privileged roles for them, but in the initial phases, when you don't have enough inputs, use the same role (SAP_ALL minus security). You can use STUSERTRACE for building interface user roles. Refer - STUSERTRACE users 

  3. Expired / Terminated Users with Role Assignments - SAP checks for user IDs that are expired or terminated but still have active role assignments. Locking a user is not enough, remove the role assignments too. It demonstrates that your offboarding process is complete, not just half-done. Have an admin process documented for this and make sure it's actually running even in non-prod systems.

  4. Reference User Type - SAP checks if you are using Reference users assigned to anyone without proper reason and documentation. I am sure, noone uses it, but if you still have any use case, document it or rather use General roles assigned to all composites.

3. Role Administration - This section typically has the most findings in any review. Most are avoidable if you build good habits from the start.

  1. Transaction Ranges in Roles  

    Never use transaction ranges (like MM* or FI*) in PFCG role menus. The reason is simple —> if a new transaction gets created within that range later, users automatically get access to it without any approval or awareness. Always add individual t-codes.

  2. Transactions Not Maintained via Menu 

    Every t-code in a role should be added through the Menu tab in PFCG, not manually inserted into the authorization data. This maintains the relationship between the t-code and its authorization objects. If you remove a t-code from the menu later, the corresponding objects get cleaned up properly. If you bypass the menu, that cleanup doesn't happen — you're left with orphaned objects giving access nobody asked for.

  3. Non-Existing Transactions in Roles

    SAP checks for t-codes in roles that don't actually exist in the system. These are usually leftovers from ECC role copies where certain transactions don't exist in S/4HANA, or from template roles that were never cleaned up. Remove the ghost tcodes once and for all (even in the non production system roles).

  4. Manual Objects in Roles

    This is a big one. Every authorization object in a role should come from SU24 proposals; triggered by adding a t-code via the menu. If you manually add objects directly in the authorization data tab, SAP loses the ability to trace the relationship between that object and the transaction that needs it. Practical Consequence is if you ever remove that t-code, the manually added objects stay behind and keep giving access. And during upgrades or SU24 changes, those manual objects don't get updated automatically. 

  5. Org. Access Maintained at Node Level

    Organizational field values (Company Code, Plant, Sales Org, etc.) should be maintained through the proper derivation mechanism, via the "Organizational Data" tab in PFCG. Not directly in the authorization data. If you edit them at the node level, the values get corrupted. Look for such values, fix it.

  6. Incorrect / Non-Existing Org Values -

    SAP also looks for org values in roles that don't exist in the system anymore. Old company codes, retired plants, restructured sales orgs. These don't break anything immediately. Run a periodic cleanup and remove obsolete org values. A quick comparison of values in AGR_1252 against the actual org data would give you the culprits.

  7. Display Roles with Maintenance Access

    If a role's name contains DISP or REPORT, SAP expects it to have only display/read access. If it also has write activities such as create, change, delete, post etc., that's a problem.

  8. Not Generated Roles

    After any change to a role, it needs to be regenerated in PFCG. An ingenerated role means users assigned to it get no access at all.

  9. Roles with Open Authorizations

    Open authorization nodes are objects in the role with no values maintained, means they don't grant access, but they create confusion and make roles hard to audit. Deactivate the nodes you don't need, fill in the values for the ones you do, and regenerate.

  10. Inconsistent Master-Derived Roles

    SAP checks if derived roles have diverged from their master. Different field values, or worse, the master role no longer exists. This breaks the whole point of the derivation concept and can lead to excess access being granted unintentionally.

4. Fiori Launchpad Administration - SAP gives a lot of attention to FIORI in S4HANA secuirty design reviews. This area surprised me as it felt more like UX administration than security. But from SAP's perspective - its both UX and Security.

  1. Use of FIORI Groups

    The FIORI groups concept is obsolete/deprecated as of SAP S/4HANA 2021. So, if you have older groups created, try to shift to Catalogs, but still you can justify the groups. But dont create any new group at all.

  2. Unused Catalogs and Groups

    Any catalog not assigned to a PFCG role is invisible to users. It's just dead weight in the system. Clean them up or assign them. SAP is firmly in the Spaces and Pages world now. If you're still using groups, plan your migration. Don't go live with a legacy Fiori structure.

  3. Enterprise Search Authorizations

    One of the most overlooked items :). People often end up maintaining S_ESH_CONN as * for all the fields. This is both a performance and Security issue. Try to maintain the proper template name and connection ID.

  4. SAP Standard Business Catalogs and Roles

    Never assign SAP standard Business Roles or catalogs directly to users or PFCG roles. Always copy them into your customer namespace, adjust to your org needs and then assign. This is both a security and a maintainability best practice.

  5. Orphaned Catalogs and Groups

    Catalogs or groups that exist but aren't assigned anywhere are just clutter. Clean them unless you have an active ongoing project reason to keep them. The same applies to orphaned tiles in Fiori Pages, where tiles pointing to catalog entries that no longer exist. These confuse users and clutter your Fiori setup.

  6. System Alias Consistency

    Fiori apps rely on system aliases to find the correct backend. If an alias is in error or has warnings, the associated OData service can't register, and the app simply won't work. Check this using SM59 or /UI2/GW_SYS_ALI and fix any discrepancies. Don't let alias issues go into production; they're hard to diagnose for end users.

  7. Granularity of Catalogs

    SAP doesn't want you to create one catalog per app. Even 2-3 is considered a bad design. Atleast 5 is a good number, if you don't want to get highlighted 🙂

  8. Service Activation

    Every Fiori app depends on an activated OData service. If the service isn't active or is in error, the app simply won't load, no error message that makes sense to the user, just a broken tile. Review all roles and catalogs and check service status. Implement a periodic control to catch regressions after transports.

  9. OData Metadata Cache

    Schedule report /UI5/UPD_ODATA_METADATA_CACHE. In test systems, hourly is fine. In production, once per night is the recommendation. This directly affects how fast Fiori apps load. It also needs to be run after importing transports, implementing SAP notes, or changing a service. Make it part of your go-live checklist.

  10. SAP Menu Entry Cache

    Similarly, run /UI2/EAM_BUILD_CACHE to cache SAP menu entries in the App Finder. Without it, the App Finder either loads very slowly or times out. If the report isn't available in your system, check whether you need to install the required SAP_UI patch level or SAP Note 2545066.

5. Custom Transactions

  1. Unused Custom Transactions

    SAP checks for Y* and Z* transactions that exist in the system but aren't in any role menu. These are typically leftovers from earlier development, retired features, or transactions that were built but never formally deployed. Lock or remove the ones you don't need. Unused custom t-codes sitting in the system are a quiet compliance risk, they can be executed by anyone with broad access, and nobody is tracking them.

  2. Custom Transactions Not Maintained in SU24

    Well, Well, Well, this is the item, which I keep iterating to ABAP teams and the client. Every custom program should have an AUTHORITY-CHECK statement, and not just for the namesake, it should check the most relevant Auth objects, for example, if a custom report is built to get the sales orders report, it should check the objects which are checked by VA03 tcode, if not all, at least few of those. Now, you can remediate this one by just adding a custom obj check in SU24 and roles, but it wont remediate the underlying vulnerability. Ideal flow should be Appropriate checks in Program --> maintained in SU24 --> Added in roles --> Added in GRC risk library (functions). 

6. Authorization-Based SAP S/4HANA Enterprise Management (Licensing) - 

This is the section most teams don't think about, while creating roles. But SAP does. Certain authorization objects automatically trigger Full User Equivalent (FUE) licensing, meaning any user with those objects in their roles gets charged at the highest license tier. The objects that typically come up are things like S_CALENDAR, S_PSE_ADM, S_TRANSPRT, S_USER_GRP, and S_SECPOL etc. 

Refer note - 3113382 - Authorization-based SAP S/4HANA User Simulation / FUE Projection.

7. Miscellaneous — Security Profile Parameters - SAP checks quite a few security, rfc, password related parameters if you have an on-premise system. But if you are on RISE, most of these are taken care and SAP checks these parameters -

auth/check/calltransaction — Controls whether authority checks are enforced during CALL TRANSACTION statements. Recommended value is 3. If it's not set correctly, authorization checks can be silently bypassed in background and batch processes.

auth/object_disabling_active — Controls whether authorization checks can be globally switched off for selected objects. Recommended value is N. If this is active, someone with the right access can effectively turn off security for entire authorization objects system-wide. It should be N in any production system.

auth/rfc_authority_check — Controls how authentication is enforced for RFC function module calls. Recommended value is 6. Anything lower means remote function calls can be made without proper authorization checks — essentially leaving a door open for unauthenticated access via RFC. This one is often set to a lower value during development for convenience, and then forgotten. Get your Basis team to set it to 6 before go-live.

 

Final thought

The review itself is not the hard part. The hard part is the daily discipline during the project like maintaining SU24, following the menu-based role building approach, keeping org values clean, not letting SAP_ALL sit on users "temporarily". These are all habits, and they're much easier to build early than to fix under review pressure.

Treat your QA system like production. If you won't, SAP certainly will.

entry.title: Re: Source Layer of “Your session has expired” Message in...
entry.content:
I suspect this is your OAuth access and refresh tokens. 
You can review the configuration on SAP Mobile Services cockpit , in your app security section. These settings should also reflect the time set on your iDP provider. 

When the OAuth Token expires it uses the refresh token to retrieve a new OAuth token, however after the refresh token expires it forces the User to log back in via the iDP to get new tokens..

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Discovery Center: From Inspiration to Guidance
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is here! This year’s event brings innovations and updates to SAP technologies like never before. Not only are the technologies evolving rapidly, but the business impact we will see across industries is equally impressive.

For inspiration and guidance on translating your vision from what’s possible with these technologies to how you can achieve maximum results, SAP Discovery Center is your resource. For evaluating new technologies or expanding current solutions, the Discovery Center resources help you move from exploration to implementation faster and with less guesswork.

If you are new to SAP Discovery Center, watch this quick overview video to get started.

SAP Discovery Center Home Page

The latest release brings new enhancements to Discovery Center, designed to speed up your journey from simple exploration to impactful adoption.

Let’s dive into what’s new for SAP Discovery Center.

New SAP Business Data Cloud Service Catalog

Explore SAP Business Data Cloud Services in one central place. Discovery Center has expanded its catalog offerings with a new catalog for SAP BDC Services. Get instant access to missions, roadmaps, and guidance for managing SAP and non-SAP data. Review transparent pricing and practical examples of how these services solve your business challenges. You can easily move from initial discovery to solution adoption with expert guidance and direction.

SAP Business Data Cloud Service Catalog

 Explore the SAP Business Data Cloud Services Catalog and start a mission.

New Maturity Assessments for SAP Business AI

Introduced at last year’s SAP TechEd, Maturity Assessments are comprehensive technical evaluations that transform uncertainty into a prioritized investment roadmap. In just 10-15 minutes, you'll identify platform or AI maturity gaps and benchmark against industry peers.

SAP BTP Adoption Framework Landing Page

We have since expanded the portfolio with the new Line of Business AI assessments for domain-specific insights, plus an AI Maturity Calculator developed with IDC. The AI Maturity Calculator benchmarks your AI readiness against your industry, helping you understand where you stand across three maturity levels: Observing, Advancing, and Leading. 

Learn more about the AI execution gap and current IDC research in this blog post: Most companies have an AI strategy. Here’s what the top 8% do differently.

Take a free assessment and try the AI Maturity Calculator.

A Refreshed User Experience

With a fresh home page design, updated icons and colors, SAP Discovery Center makes site navigation and orientation easier. Each catalog features its own color scheme and related icons.

Mission Catalog

Experience the new design on the home page and in the Mission Catalog.

New AI Offerings

AI Agent: Project Billing Verification Agent (Beta)

The Project Billing Price Verification Agent in the Manage Project Billing application of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition helps billing specialists quickly identify mismatches between agreed prices and billing amounts.

AI Feature: SAP Digital Manufacturing, Joule

The conversational search feature with Joule provides faster, smoother, contextual access to SAP Help Portal content summarized in a clear answer to the question.

AI Feature: SAP Integrated Product Development, Joule

Joule Basic Navigation to the initial page of each IPD application, such as “Open My Collaboration app”, “Open Manage Specification app” etc.

New Missions

Activate Joule for SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition

This mission guides you through the setup of SAP Joule with your SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud instance. Joule revolutionizes how you interact with SAP business systems, making every touchpoint count and every task simpler.

Activate free SAP Build - Test, Demo and Development for SAP Partners

This mission helps SAP Partners to activate the free SAP Build test, demo, and development (TDD) licenses. SAP provides free access to SAP Build test, demo, and development (TDD) licenses for all SAP partners. This enables partners to innovate on SAP Build up to fair usage limits for customer workshops, proofs of concepts, building accelerators.

Partner Mission: High Availability Architecture for the UX/HANA Cloud Business Critical apps - (clean core)

When designing SAP BTP User Experience services, it is imperative the architecture has resilience in the case of service disruption, especially when the applications could be business critical. This mission provides option(s) to architect a high availability solution across two providers.

SAP BTP Guidance Framework Becomes SAP BTP Adoption Framework

Same expert guidance with a sharper focus on getting you from planning to production faster.

Get started with the SAP BTP Adoption Framework assets.

Become a SAP Discovery Center Insider

Pro tip: Stay up to date with what’s new in SAP Discovery Center (features and content) via the ‘What’s New & Updates’ page.

What's New & Updates

Recommended Sessions at ASUG/SAP Sapphire

Your platform journey: Maturity assessment and adoption best practices | BTP1549

Explore the new maturity assessment that benchmarks your current state and uncovers optimization opportunities, paired with the proven platform adoption framework for SAP BTP that guides implementation.  

🗓️ Add to schedule

Real-life stories: turning missions into values with SAP Discovery Center | BTP2032

See how Agilent Technologies and Sutherland Global Services have turned ideas into production-ready solutions with SAP Business Technology Platform using missions in SAP Discovery Center. Hear them share their experience from real use cases, practical implementation steps, and key lessons learned, so you can start delivering business value fast.

🗓️ Add to schedule

Use cases to maximize your platform investment | BTP1344

Unlock the next level of value in SAP's platform by exploring high-impact use cases for automation, app extensions, and agentic AI that can be activated using your entitlements in SAP BTP, including the SAP Build solution. Through practical scenarios, see how SAP's platform can help accelerate innovation and turn business challenges into business opportunities.

🗓️ Add to schedule

Explore the strategy and road map for SAP’s unified platform | BTP1202

Discover why SAP's platform is the foundation of choice for SAP customers. Explore its core components and how they enable business success, then get a sneak peek at the latest and upcoming innovations and enhancements planned in the months ahead.

🗓️ Add to schedule

You May Also be Interested in

SAP BTP Garage Webinar Series

Elevate your skills in the SAP BTP Garage. A monthly webinar series showcasing priority use cases and hands-on demos. Meet the experts and get answers to your top questions.

Register for the series

entry.title: Re: Introducing Expanded Usage of the Reference Capability Model
entry.content:
Hi @SreelathaN.

I'm a big fan of the Reference Business Architecture that SAP has developed and our business embraced it. I’m a little unclear on the use of the term “Reference Capability Model” and the attached spreadsheet in your post.

I’m familiar with both the SAP Reference Business Capability Model and the proposed SAP Reference Technical Capability Model, so I’d appreciate clarification on what is meant here. Is the “Reference Capability Model” intended to represent a combination of these two layers?

The spreadsheet attached to the post introduces a number of Business Domains that don’t align with the Reference Business Capability Model as it currently appears across SAP tooling. In particular, the items highlighted in yellow (in my image below of that sheets Business Domains) don’t seem to exist within the standard model I’m familiar with e.g. Application Development and Automation, Artificial Intelligence, Data and Analytics, Integration, IT Administration, IT Security, Solution Characteristics are not part of the model I'm familiar with. Also, the Business Domain "IT Management" is missing from the spreadsheet.

If these additional domains are indeed being incorporated, it raises a concern around mutual exclusivity, which is a core principle of a well-structured capability model. For example, IT Security already appears to be comprehensively covered within the Governance, Risk, and Compliance domain (e.g., Cyber Security Management, Data Privacy Management, Data Protection, Risk Management, Controls Management, Access Governance, Authentication Management, Identity Management, etc.). Introducing it as a separate domain would create overlap and ambiguity.

Could you please clarify how these additional domains are intended to fit within the model, and whether the expectation is to maintain strict mutual exclusivity across the taxonomy?
